gbtk
  • Lattice module: lattice.py
  • View page source

Lattice module: lattice.py

[1]:

import os
import sys
module_path = os.path.abspath(os.path.join('../../..'))
if module_path not in sys.path:
    sys.path.append(module_path)
import gbtk.lattice as lattice

This module contains a definition of a crystal lattice. It holds an array that defines the edges of the three sets of sides of a parallelipiped (vectors in rows), an array holding the fractional coordinates of the atoms in the unit cell and an array to store the types of the atoms.

Not all lattice types are currently supported. You can find out which by running the following command:

[2]:

lattice.print_supported_types()

currently supported lattice types are:
['fcc', 'bcc', 'sc', 'fcc-bct', 'hcp', 'hcp-ortho', 'CsCl', 'ZrO2-fct']

Define and visualise a lattice

Making use of this module is simply a matter of creating a lattice of a given type.

[3]:

test_lattice = lattice.Lattice('fcc')

[4]:

test_lattice.print_lattice_spec()

Details of lattice
------------------
Lattice type:
fcc

Cell vectors:
[[1. 0. 0.]
 [0. 1. 0.]
 [0. 0. 1.]]

Number of atoms in basis:
4

Atom types:
[1 1 1 1]

Number of atom types
1
Positions of atoms
[[0.  0.  0. ]
 [0.5 0.5 0. ]
 [0.  0.5 0.5]
 [0.5 0.  0.5]]

or visualise the lattice unit cell in a 3D plot:

[5]:

test_lattice.visualise_3d()

Some lattice types require extra parameters or can be specified in several ways

For example, specifying the hcp lattice with no extra parameters will give a lattice with an ideal c/a ratio by default:”

[6]:

test_lattice = lattice.Lattice('hcp')

Alternatively, we can provide one value for the length parameter to explicitly specify the c/a ratio:

[7]:

test_lattice = lattice.Lattice('hcp', lengths=[5.0])

We have an additional option for the hexagonal close packed lattice: we can specify a four-atom orthogonal unit cell via the lattice type ‘hcp-ortho’. Note that the atomic positions in this unit cell may look a little unusual. This is because we hae chosen them so that the origin is at a centre of inversion symmetry. This is important under certain circumstances when working with bicrystals.

[8]:

test_lattice = lattice.Lattice('hcp-ortho')
test_lattice.visualise_3d()

[ ]:
